# Diffusion Models {.section-divider}

## One Recipe, Many Data Types

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
Diffusion models do not fundamentally care whether the sample is:

- an image
- a video
- a robot trajectory
- an action chunk

The object is represented as a tensor and modeled as a sample from a data distribution.
:::

::: {}
![](assets/figures/tensor_modalities.svg){width=100%}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: start from the generality of the diffusion recipe before specializing to actions.

The useful insight from Vincenzo's lecture material is that diffusion operates on tensors. An image, a video, and a robot trajectory have different structure, but in each case we can represent the object as a point in a high-dimensional space.

For robotics, this matters because the model can generate not only pixels, but also vectors of joint commands, end-effector poses, target displacements, or controller parameters. The neural architecture changes with the structure of the tensor, but the noising and denoising idea remains the same.

Warning: this does not mean the semantics are irrelevant for the whole system. Semantics enter through the architecture, the conditioning information, the dataset, and the controller that executes the output.
:::

---

## Data as Tensors

![](assets/photos/chaos2clarity_input_data_modalities.png){width=96% fig-align="center"}

::: {.takeaway}
The same generative objective can be applied to images, videos, and robot trajectories once they are represented as high-dimensional arrays.
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: make the tensor abstraction concrete before introducing the diffusion equations.

The slide before this one gives the abstract statement: diffusion models operate on tensors. This visual makes the statement less abstract. An image has height, width, and color channels; a video adds a time axis; a robot trajectory adds a sequence axis and action or configuration dimensions.

For the rest of the lecture, this is the bridge from image diffusion to policy diffusion. We are going to reuse the same noising and denoising machinery, but the object being generated will become an action chunk rather than an image.
:::

---

## Diffusion as Distribution Learning

Generation means sampling a structured object:

::: {.equation-card}
$$
x_0 \sim p_{\text{data}}(x)
$$
:::

Diffusion replaces direct sampling from $p_{\text{data}}$ with an easier path:

::: {.process-list}
1. define a fixed process that destroys structure with Gaussian noise
2. train a neural network to reverse that process
3. sample by iteratively denoising random noise
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: formalize generation as sampling from the data distribution.

We do not know the true distribution of demonstrations, images, or action chunks. We only have samples. A diffusion model learns a reverse process that starts from a distribution we can sample easily, usually a standard Gaussian, and gradually moves toward the data distribution.

This framing will later become a policy: instead of sampling an unconditional object, we sample an action trajectory conditioned on observations.
:::

---

## Forward and Reverse Processes

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
**Forward process:** fixed Gaussian corruption

$$
q(x_k \mid x_{k-1})
=
\mathcal{N}
\left(
x_k;
\sqrt{1-\beta_k}x_{k-1},
\beta_k I
\right)
$$
:::

::: {.compact}
**Reverse process:** learned conditional denoising

$$
p_\theta(x_{k-1} \mid x_k, c)
$$

- Forward: progressively destroy structure.
- Reverse: reconstruct structure step by step.
:::
:::

![](assets/photos/chaos2clarity_diffusion_forward_reverse.png){width=96% fig-align="center"}

::: {.takeaway}
In Diffusion Policy, the same picture applies in **action-chunk space** instead of pixel space.
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: separate the fixed corruption process from the learned denoising process, and make the direction of the two processes visually obvious.

The forward process is not learned. We choose a schedule of noise variances and progressively corrupt the data. The reverse process is learned because the true reverse conditional depends on the unknown data distribution.

I use $k$ for diffusion time. This is not robot time. Robot time indexes physical execution, while diffusion time indexes the artificial denoising loop used by the generative model. For image diffusion, we can see the corruption directly. For robot actions, the samples are vectors or trajectories, so the visual intuition is less obvious, but the math is the same: a clean expert action chunk is corrupted during training, and the model learns how to remove the artificial noise.
:::

---

## Closed-Form Noising

By composing the forward steps, we can sample directly at any diffusion step:

::: {.equation-card}
$$
x_k
=
\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_k}x_0
+
\sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_k}\epsilon,
\qquad
\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0,I)
$$
:::

where

$$
\alpha_k = 1-\beta_k,
\qquad
\bar{\alpha}_k = \prod_{i=1}^{k}\alpha_i .
$$

Equivalently, the marginal is a single Gaussian centered on a shrinking copy of the clean sample:

$$
q(x_k \mid x_0)
=
\mathcal{N}\!\left(
x_k;\;
\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_k}\,x_0,\;
(1-\bar{\alpha}_k)I
\right)
$$

::: {.takeaway}
Training does not require simulating all previous noising steps. We can draw $k$ and corrupt $x_0$ in one step.
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: the closed-form forward marginal makes training efficient.

This is one of the most important practical facts. If we want a noisy sample at diffusion step $k$, we do not have to run the full chain from $0$ to $k$. We sample Gaussian noise once and mix it with the clean sample using $\bar{\alpha}_k$.

For action trajectories, $x_0$ is the expert action chunk. We corrupt that chunk and ask the network to predict the noise that was added.
:::

---

## Noise Prediction Objective

A standard training objective predicts the injected noise:

::: {.equation-card}
$$
\mathcal{L}_{diff}(\theta)
=
\mathbb{E}_{x_0,\epsilon,k}
\left[
\lVert
\epsilon - \epsilon_\theta(x_k, k, c)
\rVert^2
\right]
$$
:::

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
- $x_0$: clean data sample or expert action chunk
- $x_k$: noisy version at diffusion step $k$
- $c$: optional conditioning information
- $\epsilon_\theta$: denoising network prediction
:::

::: {.warning}
The model is not trained to "undo robot noise." The noise is a learning construction used to fit a generative model.
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: the model learns to estimate the noise component added during the forward process.

Once the model can estimate the noise in $x_k$, we can use that estimate to move the sample toward a cleaner point. The same loss is used whether $x$ is an image tensor or an action trajectory tensor.

There are other parameterizations, such as predicting $x_0$ or a velocity variable, but noise prediction is the standard formulation to introduce here.
:::

---

## What the Denoiser Learns

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
- The network sees a noisy sample and a diffusion step.
- It predicts the noise component that should be removed.
- Conditioning $c$ tells the network which sample is relevant for the current context.

$$
\epsilon_\theta(x_k, k, c)
$$
:::

::: {}
![](assets/photos/chaos2clarity_trained_denoiser_cartoon.png){width=100%}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: connect the loss to a concrete neural-network computation.

The denoiser is not a generic image classifier. It is trained to answer a very specific question: given this noisy sample, at this diffusion step, and under this conditioning context, what noise was added?

At inference time, the answer is used to update the sample toward a cleaner one. In Diffusion Policy, the same denoiser operates on action trajectories rather than pixels, and the conditioning is the robot observation history.
:::

---

## Why the Reverse Step Is Hard

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
The step we would like to sample, $q(x_{k-1}\mid x_k)$, is **intractable**:

$$
q(x_{k-1}\mid x_k)
=
\frac{q(x_k\mid x_{k-1})\,q(x_{k-1})}{q(x_k)}
$$

Both marginals depend on the unknown data distribution $q(x_0)$.
:::

::: {.compact}
But **conditioned on $x_0$** it is a tractable Gaussian:

$$
q(x_{k-1}\mid x_k, x_0)
=
\mathcal{N}\!\left(x_{k-1};\ \tilde{\mu}_k,\ \tilde{\beta}_k I\right)
$$

$$
\tilde{\mu}_k
=
\frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha_k}}
\left(
x_k - \frac{1-\alpha_k}{\sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_k}}\,\epsilon
\right),
\quad
\tilde{\beta}_k
=
\frac{1-\bar{\alpha}_{k-1}}{1-\bar{\alpha}_k}\,\beta_k
$$
:::
:::

::: {.warning}
At generation time $x_0$ is exactly what we are trying to produce. So we train a network $p_\theta(x_{k-1}\mid x_k, c)$ to *approximate* this posterior.
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: motivate why a neural network is needed at all, instead of just inverting the forward process analytically.

This is the rigorous backbone behind the noise-prediction loss. It can be delivered quickly or treated as optional depending on the audience, but it answers the natural question "why not just reverse the Gaussian?".

The true single-step reverse conditional is intractable because, through Bayes' rule, it depends on the marginal $q(x_k)$, which in turn depends on the unknown $q(x_0)$. The key trick from the DDPM derivation is that, once we additionally condition on the clean sample $x_0$, the reverse conditional becomes a closed-form Gaussian whose mean $\tilde{\mu}_k$ and variance $\tilde{\beta}_k$ are known functions of the noise schedule.

The mean $\tilde{\mu}_k$ is exactly the quantity the model will learn to match. Notation follows our diffusion-time index $k$; the derivation is adapted from Vincenzo Pomponi's lecture material [@pomponi2026chaos2clarity] and the original DDPM paper [@ho2020ddpm].
:::

---

## From Likelihood to the Loss

::: {.compact}
We want to maximize the data likelihood $\log p_\theta(x_0)$, but the marginal is intractable. Using the forward process and Jensen's inequality gives a tractable **Evidence Lower Bound**:
:::

::: {.equation-card}
$$
\log p_\theta(x_0)
\;\ge\;
\mathcal{L}_{\text{ELBO}}
=
\mathbb{E}_q\!\left[
\log \frac{p_\theta(x_{0:K})}{q(x_{1:K}\mid x_0)}
\right]
$$
:::

It decomposes into per-step KL divergences between the two Gaussians above:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{ELBO}}
=
\underbrace{\mathbb{E}_q\big[\log p_\theta(x_0\mid x_1)\big]}_{\text{reconstruction}}
-
\sum_{k\ge 2}
D_{\mathrm{KL}}\!\big(
q(x_{k-1}\mid x_k, x_0)\,\|\,p_\theta(x_{k-1}\mid x_k)
\big)
+ \text{const}
$$

::: {.takeaway}
Each Gaussian KL reduces to $\frac{1}{2\tilde{\beta}_k}\lVert\tilde{\mu}_k - \mu_\theta(x_k,k)\rVert^2$, which — after reparameterizing the mean through the noise — recovers the simple noise-prediction loss $\lVert\epsilon - \epsilon_\theta(x_k,k,c)\rVert^2$.
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: close the loop between the probabilistic derivation and the simple squared-error loss introduced earlier.

The full ELBO is the same object that appears in variational autoencoders. The important conceptual steps are: (1) we cannot maximize the true log-likelihood, (2) we maximize a lower bound instead, (3) the bound becomes a sum of KL divergences between Gaussians, which are available in closed form, and (4) matching the means is equivalent, up to a per-step weight, to predicting the injected noise.

In practice the weighting is dropped and the constant terms are ignored, which is exactly the $\lVert\epsilon - \epsilon_\theta\rVert^2$ objective from the previous slide. This is why noise prediction is not an arbitrary heuristic: it is the simplified ELBO [@ho2020ddpm]. Derivation adapted from [@pomponi2026chaos2clarity].
:::

---

## Architecture Depends on Structure

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
The loss can stay the same while the denoising backbone changes:

- 2D U-Net for image tensors
- 1D U-Net for temporal action chunks
- Transformers for longer-range coupling

For Diffusion Policy, the key transition is from spatial denoising to sequence denoising.
:::

::: {}
![](assets/figures/diffusion_architecture_bridge.svg){width=100%}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: motivate the 1D U-Net used later in Diffusion Policy.

The diffusion objective only says that the network receives a noisy sample and a diffusion step, then predicts noise of the same shape. It does not prescribe the architecture.

The architecture should match the structure of the data. Images have local spatial structure, so a 2D U-Net is natural. Action trajectories have temporal structure, so a 1D U-Net with convolutions along the horizon is natural. Transformers can be useful when longer-range interactions dominate, but they usually require more data and compute.
:::

---

## Sampling Bottleneck

Sampling starts from Gaussian noise and applies the denoiser repeatedly:

::: {.equation-card}
$$
x_K \sim \mathcal{N}(0,I),
\qquad
x_K \rightarrow x_{K-1} \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow x_0
$$
:::

- More denoising steps usually give better refinement.
- Each step is a neural network evaluation.
- Robot policies must respect a control-loop timing budget.

::: {.notes}
Main point: introduce the computational bottleneck before discussing DDPM and DDIM.

This is where diffusion models become different from a standard feed-forward policy. A deterministic policy typically performs one forward pass per control query. A diffusion policy may require tens or hundreds of denoising passes to produce one action chunk.

For offline image generation, this may be acceptable. For robot control, latency and jitter affect closed-loop behavior, tracking error, and contact response.
:::

---

## DDPM Sampling

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
Start from noise $x_K \sim \mathcal{N}(0,I)$ and walk back step by step. The predicted noise fixes the reverse mean:

$$
\mu_\theta(x_k,k)
=
\frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha_k}}
\left(
x_k - \frac{1-\alpha_k}{\sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_k}}\,\epsilon_\theta(x_k,k,c)
\right)
$$

Then sample the next, less noisy state:

$$
x_{k-1} = \mu_\theta(x_k,k) + \sqrt{\tilde{\beta}_k}\,z,
\qquad z \sim \mathcal{N}(0,I)
$$

- $\mu_\theta$: deterministic denoising direction
- $\sqrt{\tilde{\beta}_k}\,z$: stochastic exploration term
:::

::: {}
![](assets/figures/ddpm_sampling_loop.svg){width=92%}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: make the abstract reverse loop concrete as the original DDPM ancestral sampler.

This is the same $\mu_\theta$ that the ELBO told us to match. The sampler simply plugs the network's noise prediction into the closed-form reverse mean and then adds calibrated Gaussian noise with variance $\tilde{\beta}_k$.

The flowchart on the right is the full algorithm: sample noise, loop $k = K \ldots 1$ predicting noise, computing the mean, and sampling the next state, then return $x_0$. The stochastic term is why DDPM usually needs many steps to produce clean samples [@ho2020ddpm].
:::

---

## DDIM Sampling

::: {.compact}
DDIM reuses the *same trained network* but rewrites the reverse step. First read off an estimate of the clean sample:
:::

::: {.equation-card}
$$
\hat{x}_0
=
\frac{1}{\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_k}}
\left(
x_k - \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_k}\,\epsilon_\theta(x_k,k,c)
\right)
$$
:::

then move along a non-Markovian trajectory with a controllable noise level $\sigma_k$:

$$
x_{k-1}
=
\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_{k-1}}\,\hat{x}_0
+
\sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_{k-1}-\sigma_k^2}\;\epsilon_\theta(x_k,k,c)
+
\sigma_k z
$$

::: {.takeaway}
$\sigma_k$ is a free knob. With $\sigma_k = 0$ sampling is **deterministic** and can skip many intermediate steps — attractive for meeting a control-loop budget.
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: DDIM is a different sampler, not a different model — no retraining is needed.

The first equation is exactly the $\hat{x}_0$ readout implied by the closed-form forward process. The second equation reconstructs $x_{k-1}$ from this estimate plus a controllable amount of injected noise.

When $\sigma_k = 0$ the whole trajectory is determined by the initial noise and the network outputs, so we can take large steps and still land on a coherent sample. This is the main reason DDIM-style samplers appear in real-time diffusion policies, where each denoising step costs one network evaluation [@song2021ddim].
:::
